text.title: AWS Service Selection: Serverless vs Containers
text.summary: Decision criteria for choosing Lambda, ECS/Fargate, or EKS, with a hybrid-by-default stance for 2025-2026.

rule.statement: Choose Lambda when traffic is variable or spiky, execution is under 15 minutes, cold start is acceptable, and GPU is not required.
rule.rationale: Lambda costs per invocation; idle time costs nothing. Containers cost per provisioned hour regardless of traffic.

rule.statement: Choose containers (EKS/ECS/Fargate) for steady 24/7 workloads, long-running processes, GPU requirements, or when Kubernetes ecosystem is needed.
rule.rationale: Reserved container capacity is cheaper than Lambda at high sustained throughput. GPU workloads require EC2-backed compute.

rule.statement: Default to a hybrid architecture: Lambda for event-driven and sporadic workloads, containers for always-on services.
rule.rationale: 78% of engineering teams run hybrid architectures. The question is which workload belongs where, not a binary choice.

example.note: E-commerce: EKS for the main web API (steady traffic), Lambda for order notifications (spiky), Step Functions for payment workflow (stateful).

antipattern.description: Running all workloads as Lambda including a websocket server or ML inference service.
antipattern.reason: Lambda has a 15-minute timeout, no persistent connections, and no GPU support.

rule.statement: Choose Fargate (ECS or EKS) when the team lacks Kubernetes expertise or wants to avoid node management overhead.
rule.rationale: Fargate is fully managed; no patching, scaling, or capacity planning for nodes.

rule.statement: Choose EKS when multi-cloud portability, Helm ecosystem, or advanced scheduling is required.
rule.rationale: EKS uses standard Kubernetes APIs, enabling migration to other clouds or on-prem clusters.

rule.statement: Use RDS Aurora for relational OLTP workloads; use DynamoDB for key-value or document access patterns requiring single-digit millisecond latency at any scale.
rule.rationale: Aurora provides Aurora PostgreSQL compatibility with auto-scaling storage. DynamoDB avoids connection pooling limits at high concurrency.

rule.statement: Use EventBridge for decoupled cross-service routing; use Step Functions for stateful orchestration; use SQS for async buffering with retry; use SNS for fan-out notifications.
rule.rationale: Each service solves a distinct coordination problem. Using SQS for routing or SNS for stateful workflows creates operational complexity.

rule.statement: Apply all six Well-Architected pillars: Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, Sustainability.
rule.rationale: Skipping any pillar creates technical debt: missing Security leads to breaches; missing Cost Optimization leads to uncontrolled spend.

rule.statement: Default to Graviton (ARM64) instances for all new compute resources; justify x86 explicitly.
rule.rationale: Graviton provides 20% lower cost and comparable or better performance for most workloads.
